content: {"notes": [{"url": "https://www.exonpublications.com/index.php/exon/article/view/intermittent-fasting-methods-health-benefits-risks", "title": "Intermittent Fasting Methods, Health Benefits, and Risks", "summary": "Intermittent Fasting Methods, Health Benefits, and Risks Main Article Content Intermittent fasting is an eating pattern that alternates between periods of eating and fasting. This comprehensive guide explores the principles behind intermittent fasting, various methods such as alternate day fasting, the 16/8 method, and one meal a day (OMAD), among others. It explores the health benefits associated with intermittent fasting, including weight loss and improved metabolic health, while also addressing potential risks and sustainability concerns.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["DOI: https://doi.org/10.36255/intermittent-fasting-methods-health-benefits-risks Introduction Intermittent fasting has gained significant attention in recent years as a dietary strategy for weight management and overall health improvement.", "Eat-Stop-Eat (ESE) The Eat-Stop-Eat method entails fasting for a full 24 hours once or twice a week.", "The 16/8 Method The 16/8 method, also known as time-restricted feeding, involves fasting for 16 hours each day and consuming all daily calories within an 8-hour window.", "Water Fasting Water fasting involves consuming only water for a specific period, typically ranging from 24 to 72 hours.", "Juice Fasts Juice fasting entails consuming only fruit and vegetable juices for a certain period, usually 1 to 3 days."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: DOI: https://doi.org/10.36255/intermittent-fasting-methods-health-benefits-risks Introduction Intermittent fasting has g"}, {"url": "https://www.kelsey-seybold.com/your-health-resources/blog/the-pros-and-cons-of-intermittent-fasting", "title": "Intermittent Fasting: Benefits, Risks, and What You Should Know", "summary": "Subscribe to our monthly newsletter to receive encouraging advice to help you lead a healthy lifestyle. Intermittent Fasting: Benefits, Risks, and What You Should Know Intermittent fasting has become a popular approach for weight loss, improved metabolism, and general health. But before you commit, it\u2019s important to weigh the pros and cons of intermittent fasting to determine if it\u2019s the right fit for your lifestyle.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["The most popular methods include the 16/8 fasting schedule (fasting for 16 hours and eating during an 8-hour window), alternate-day fasting, and the 5:2 diet.", "The difference between an eating pattern and a diet is that an eating pattern guides timing and structure, while a diet typically prescribes specific foods or calorie counts.", "Cons - Extended fasting periods may cause irritability and fatigue. - May be challenging for those with busy schedules or early morning workouts. - Risk of overeating during the eating window."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: The most popular methods include the 16/8 fasting schedule (fasting for 16 hours and eating during an 8-hour window), al"}, {"url": "https://www.mdpi.com/2072-6643/14/19/4216", "title": "Intermittent fasting\u2014short-and long-term quality of life, fatigue, and safety in healthy volunteers: a prospective, clinical trial", "summary": "Intermittent Fasting\u2014Short- and Long-Term Quality of Life, Fatigue, and Safety in Healthy Volunteers: A Prospective, Clinical Trial Abstract 1. Short Form Health 36 (SF-36) Fatigue 2.4. Safety Profile and Metabolic Changes 3.4.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Short Form Health 36 (SF-36) Fatigue 2.4.", "Safety Profile and Metabolic Changes 3.4.", "Nutr. 2017, 37, 371\u2013393. [Google Scholar] [CrossRef] [PubMed] - De Toledo, F.W.; Grundler, F.; Sirtori, C.R.; Ruscica, M."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Short Form Health 36 (SF-36) Fatigue 2.4."}, {"url": "https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12937-024-00975-9", "title": "Adverse events profile associated with intermittent fasting in adults with overweight or obesity: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials", "summary": "Abstract Background There is little evidence to comprehensively summarize the adverse events (AEs) profile of intermittent fasting (IF) despite its widespread use in patients with overweight or obesity. Methods We searched the main electronic databases and registry websites to identify eligible randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing IF versus control groups. A direct meta-analysis using a fixed-effect model was conducted to pool the risk differences regarding common AEs and dropouts.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Abstract Background There is little evidence to comprehensively summarize the adverse events (AEs) profile of intermittent fasting (IF) despite its widespread use in patients with overweight or obesity.", "Findings did not show a significant difference between IF and Control in risk rate of fatigue [0%, 95% confidence interval (CI), -1% to 2%; P = 0.61], headache [0%, 95%CI: -1% to 2%; P = 0.86] and dropout [1%, 95%CI: -2% to 4%; P = 0.51].", "However, a numerically higher risk of dizziness was noted among the IF alone subgroup with non-early time restricted eating [3%, 95%CI: -0% to 6%; P = 0.08].", "Conclusions This meta-analysis suggested that IF was not associated with a greater risk of AEs in adult patients affected by overweight or obesity.", "In China, an estimated 34.3% of the adult population is overweight with another 16.4% being obese [1]; in the USA, 33.3% of adults with obesity [2] and in Europe, 34.5% are overweight and 15.8% are obese [3]."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Abstract Background There is little evidence to comprehensively summarize the adverse events (AEs) profile of intermitte"}, {"url": "https://academic.oup.com/ajcn/article/102/2/464/4564588", "title": "Health effects of intermittent fasting: hormesis or harm? Find out more about the books migrating to Oxford Academic. - The content that you are trying to access may no longer be available.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Page Not Found Sorry, the page you requested could not be found.", "Here are a few suggestions: - The content that you are trying to access might have recently moved to Oxford Academic."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Page Not Found Sorry, the page you requested could not be found."}, {"url": "https://www.nature.com/articles/s43587-020-00013-3", "title": "Intermittent and periodic fasting, longevity and disease", "summary": "Abstract Intermittent and periodic fasting (IF and PF, respectively) are emerging as safe strategies to affect longevity and healthspan by acting on cellular aging and disease risk factors, while causing no or minor side effects. IF lasting from 12 to 48 hours and repeated every 1 to 7 days and PF lasting 2 to 7 days and repeated once per month or less have the potential to prevent and treat disease, but their effect on cellular aging and the molecular mechanisms involved are only beginning to be unraveled. Here, we describe the different fasting methods and their effect on longevity in organisms ranging from yeast to humans, linking them to the major nutrient-sensing signaling pathways and focusing on the benefits of the fasting and the refeeding periods.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Abstract Intermittent and periodic fasting (IF and PF, respectively) are emerging as safe strategies to affect longevity and healthspan by acting on cellular aging and disease risk factors, while causing no or minor side effects.", "IF lasting from 12 to 48 hours and repeated every 1 to 7 days and PF lasting 2 to 7 days and repeated once per month or less have the potential to prevent and treat disease, but their effect on cellular aging and the molecular mechanisms involved are", "USD 39.95 Prices may be subject to local taxes which are calculated during checkout Similar content being viewed by others References Fontana, L., Partridge, L. & Longo, V.", "The effect of retarded growth upon the length of life span and upon the ultimate body size. 1935.", "Dietary restriction in mice beginning at 1 year of age: effect on life-span and spontaneous cancer incidence."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Abstract Intermittent and periodic fasting (IF and PF, respectively) are emerging as safe strategies to affect longevity"}]}
